chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Chrysocolla has a disordered, poorly crystalline to amorphous structure. It is often described as a copper-bearing phyllosilicate mineraloid. The structure resembles allophane (amorphous aluminosilicate) with Cu replacing Al. Water content is variable and affects physical properties. Some authorities describe it as a mixture of copper hydroxide and amorphous silica.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The formula is highly variable and debated; Cu content, Al content, and water vary significantly between specimens. The vivid cyan/blue-green color is entirely due to Cu2+. Pure chrysocolla without impurities is relatively soft and light; when intimately intergrown with quartz or chalcedony it forms the harder gem-silica material.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Not well defined; essentially amorphous to nanocrystalline
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Chrysocolla is essentially amorphous or at best nanocrystalline. It does not form macroscopic crystals. All occurrences are massive, botryoidal, earthy, or as crusts. X-ray diffraction shows only broad, diffuse peaks. It is best classified as a mineraloid.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Hardness and SG vary with water content and admixed silica. Gem-silica (chrysocolla in chalcedony) reaches Mohs 6.5–7 and SG ~2.58. Pure earthy chrysocolla is very soft (2–2.5) and light (SG ~1.9–2.0). Water loss on heating causes cracking and color change.

color_profiles.name: cyan-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in the structure; high Cu content with low Fe.
color_profiles.conditions: Oxidized zones of copper deposits; intense cyan is most prized.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ with minor Fe or Mn shifting hue.

color_profiles.name: gem-silica
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in chalcedony host; silicification increases hardness and translucency.
color_profiles.conditions: High-silica environments; Arizona USA (Superior, Globe-Miami), Philippines.

color_profiles.name: green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: High Cu with slight oxidation state shift; mixing with malachite.
color_profiles.conditions: Often intergrown or mixed with malachite in copper oxidation zones.

common_forms: earthy blue-green coatings on copper ore
appearance.visual_notes: The vivid cyan to turquoise-blue color is unmistakable. Chrysocolla is often the most visually striking mineral in copper oxidation zones. It frequently occurs with malachite (green), azurite (deep blue), and cuprite (red), creating spectacular polychrome specimens.

optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.46–1.57 (variable; amorphous). Gem-silica RI closer to chalcedony (~1.53–1.54). Isotropic to weakly anisotropic.

geologic_environments: oxidation zones of copper sulfide deposits
notable_localities: Bisbee and Globe-Miami, Arizona, USA (gem-silica and massive chrysocolla)
Shaba Province (Katanga), Democratic Republic of Congo
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Chrysocolla is one of the most characteristic secondary copper minerals, forming wherever copper sulfides are weathered in the presence of silica-rich waters. It forms relatively early in the oxidation sequence and may be replaced over time by more stable copper minerals. Gem-silica forms when silica-rich groundwater simultaneously deposits chalcedony and chrysocolla.

historical_uses: Chrysocolla was described by Theophrastus (~315 BCE) and named from Greek chrysos (gold) + kolla (glue) — it was used as a flux for soldering gold in antiquity
Egyptian blue pigment and some green-blue decorative materials in ancient contexts may involve chrysocolla
Cleopatra reportedly wore chrysocolla jewelry; Pliny the Elder describes it in Natural History
Used as a blue-green pigment in ancient Egypt, Rome, and South America
Timna Valley, Israel: one of the world's earliest copper mining sites (active from Chalcolithic through Roman periods); chrysocolla abundant
cultural_associations: Associated with copper-working cultures worldwide — wherever copper was mined, chrysocolla occurred as a vivid blue-green indicator mineral
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The ancient Greek name means 'gold solder' — reflecting its actual historical use as a flux material in gold soldering. In modern crystal markets it is often conflated with turquoise due to color similarity.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Chrysocolla is the color of patinated copper — a vivid, slightly decayed cyan that evokes wet tropical ruins and ocean-floor chemistry. Its softness and mineraloid nature give it an impermanence that contrasts with its brilliant color.

practical_notes.toxicity: Moderate. Contains significant copper; avoid ingesting dust. Do not use in gem elixirs. Handle with normal hygiene (wash hands after handling).
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft and brittle; handle carefully. Sensitive to dehydration — avoid prolonged heat or dry conditions. Some specimens crack or fade as water is lost.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid ultrasonic, steam, and prolonged water immersion (can cause dehydration cracking). Wipe with damp cloth only. Store away from heat and very dry environments.
treatments_or_synthetics: Wax or resin impregnation common for stabilization in jewelry use
Often sold mixed with or as 'gem-silica' when chalcedony-hosted
Dyed howlite or magnesite sometimes sold as chrysocolla
common_confusions: Turquoise (harder, phosphate not silicate, different streak)
Gem-silica is chrysocolla in chalcedony — more durable variety
practical_notes.market_notes: Massive chrysocolla is common and affordable. Gem-silica (chrysocolla chalcedony) is rare and commands high prices as a cabochon gem. Mixed chrysocolla-malachite-azurite specimens from Bisbee and Congo are popular collector pieces.

prompt_fragments: vivid cyan chrysocolla crust on copper ore
earthy blue-green mineraloid in oxidation zone

provenance.notes: Formula and structural description reflect ongoing debate in mineralogical literature; the amorphous/mineraloid classification is appropriate. Copper toxicity note is precautionary and standard.
